content: Guards on the castle wall at Elsinore see the ghost of the recently dead King Hamlet. They decide they must tell his son, Prince Hamlet, about this strange appearance.

The new King Claudius addresses the court and celebrates his marriage to Queen Gertrude. Hamlet is deeply depressed about his father's death and mother's quick remarriage. Horatio tells Hamlet about the ghost.
Laertes warns his sister Ophelia not to trust Hamlet's declarations of love before leaving for France. Their father, Polonius, gives Laertes advice and orders Ophelia to stay away from Hamlet.
Hamlet joins the guards on the wall at night. The ghost appears and beckons Hamlet to follow it. Despite his friends' warnings, Hamlet follows the ghost.

The ghost reveals it is the spirit of Hamlet's father and claims he was murdered by Claudius. He demands that Hamlet seek revenge. Hamlet swears his friends to secrecy and decides to pretend to be mad.

Polonius sends a spy to check on his son Laertes in Paris. Ophelia enters, terrified, and reports that Hamlet came to her room acting completely crazy. Polonius decides Hamlet is mad with love for Ophelia.

King Claudius and Queen Gertrude welcome Hamlet's childhood friends, Rosencrantz and Guildenstern, asking them to spy on Hamlet. A group of traveling actors arrives, and Hamlet plans to have them perform a play resembling his father's murder to see if Claudius acts guilty.

Claudius and Polonius spy on a meeting between Hamlet and Ophelia. Hamlet delivers his famous 'To be, or not to be' speech, then cruelly tells Ophelia to go to a nunnery. Claudius realizes Hamlet is not just lovesick and decides to send him to England.
The actors perform the play Hamlet requested. When the murder scene happens, Claudius panics and stops the performance, proving his guilt to Hamlet and Horatio.
Claudius tries to pray for forgiveness for his crime. Hamlet finds him alone and has a chance to kill him, but hesitates, thinking that killing Claudius while praying would send his soul to heaven.

Hamlet confronts his mother in her bedroom. He hears someone hiding behind a curtain and, thinking it's Claudius, stabs through the fabric, accidentally killing Polonius. The ghost reappears to remind Hamlet of his mission.
Gertrude tells Claudius that Hamlet has killed Polonius. Claudius realizes he is in danger and decides he must send Hamlet to England immediately.
Rosencrantz and Guildenstern find Hamlet and ask him where he hid Polonius's body. Hamlet mocks them and refuses to give a straight answer.
Claudius demands to know where the body is, and Hamlet finally hints at its location. Claudius tells Hamlet he must leave for England, secretly planning to have him executed when he arrives.

On his way to the ship, Hamlet sees the army of Prince Fortinbras of Norway marching to fight for a small piece of land. Inspired by their dedication, Hamlet resolves to focus entirely on his revenge.
Ophelia has gone mad from grief over her father's death and Hamlet's rejection. Laertes returns from France, furious about his father's death, and leads a mob to the castle demanding answers.
Horatio receives a letter from Hamlet explaining that his ship was attacked by pirates and he has returned to Denmark. Hamlet asks Horatio to come to him.

Claudius convinces Laertes that Hamlet is to blame for everything. They plot to kill Hamlet in a fencing match using a poisoned sword and a poisoned drink. Gertrude arrives with the news that Ophelia has drowned.

Two gravediggers are preparing Ophelia's grave. Hamlet and Horatio talk to them, and Hamlet holds the skull of a court jester he once knew. When Ophelia's funeral procession arrives, Hamlet and Laertes fight over her grave.

During the fencing match, Laertes wounds Hamlet with the poisoned sword. In the scuffle, they switch swords, and Hamlet wounds Laertes with the poison. Gertrude accidentally drinks the poisoned wine and dies. Laertes confesses the plot, and Hamlet finally kills Claudius before dying himself. Fortinbras arrives and claims the throne.
Three witches meet in a storm and plan to encounter Macbeth after the current battle is over. They introduce the theme that things are not always what they seem.
King Duncan of Scotland hears a report from a wounded captain about the bravery of his generals, Macbeth and Banquo, in defeating a rebellion and an invading army.

The witches confront Macbeth and Banquo. They prophesy that Macbeth will become Thane of Cawdor and then King, and that Banquo's descendants will be kings. Messengers then arrive to tell Macbeth he has been made Thane of Cawdor, making him believe the rest of the prophecy.
King Duncan thanks Macbeth and Banquo for their service. Duncan announces his son Malcolm as his heir and says he will visit Macbeth's castle, planting the seed of murder in Macbeth's mind.
Lady Macbeth reads a letter from her husband about the witches' prophecy. She decides they must kill the king, fearing her husband is too kind to do what is necessary to seize the throne.
King Duncan and his followers arrive at Macbeth's castle. Lady Macbeth welcomes them warmly, hiding her murderous intentions.
Macbeth doubts the plan to kill the king, listing many reasons why it is wrong. Lady Macbeth manipulates him by questioning his manhood and courage until he agrees to go through with the murder.
Banquo and his son Fleance are restless at night. Macbeth hallucinates a bloody dagger pointing him toward Duncan's bedroom, and he goes to commit the murder.
Macbeth returns to his wife, deeply shaken and covered in blood after killing the king. He accidentally brought the murder weapons with him, so Lady Macbeth has to take them back to frame the guards.

Macduff arrives to wake the king and discovers the murder. In the chaos, Macbeth kills the guards to cover his tracks. Duncan's sons, Malcolm and Donalbain, flee for their lives, making them look guilty.
An old man and Ross discuss strange, unnatural events happening since the king's death. Macduff reports that Macbeth is going to be crowned the new King of Scotland.
Banquo suspects Macbeth murdered Duncan. Macbeth, fearing the prophecy that Banquo's children will be kings, hires assassins to kill Banquo and his son Fleance.
Macbeth and Lady Macbeth are both unhappy and anxious despite being king and queen. Macbeth hints to his wife that something bad is going to happen to Banquo, but doesn't tell her the exact plan.
The assassins attack Banquo and Fleance. They kill Banquo, but Fleance escapes into the night, leaving the prophecy unfulfilled.

At a royal banquet, Macbeth learns Fleance escaped. He then sees Banquo's ghost sitting in his chair and goes into a panicked fit. Lady Macbeth tries to calm the guests, but eventually has to end the feast early.
Hecate, the goddess of witchcraft, scolds the three witches for dealing with Macbeth without her permission and plans to use magic to lead him to his ruin.
Lennox and another lord discuss the state of Scotland, heavily implying that Macbeth is a tyrant and a murderer. They mention that Macduff has gone to England to seek help to overthrow Macbeth.

Macbeth visits the witches to learn his future. They show him apparitions that tell him to beware Macduff, that no man born of a woman can harm him, and that he will be safe until the local forest moves to his castle.
Lady Macduff is angry that her husband fled to England without her. Murderers sent by Macbeth arrive and brutally kill her and her young son.
In England, Macduff meets with Malcolm. They test each other's loyalty before agreeing to fight Macbeth. Ross arrives and delivers the devastating news that Macduff's family has been slaughtered.
A doctor and a gentlewoman observe Lady Macbeth sleepwalking. Driven mad by guilt, she constantly washes her hands, trying to scrub away imaginary bloodstains from the murders.
Scottish rebel forces gather near Birnam Wood to join the English army led by Malcolm and Macduff. They prepare to march on Macbeth's castle.
Macbeth prepares for battle, clinging to the witches' prophecies for confidence. He receives news of the approaching army but arrogantly dismisses the threat.
Malcolm orders his soldiers to cut down branches from Birnam Wood and carry them as camouflage. This makes it look like the forest is moving toward the castle, fulfilling one of the prophecies.
Macbeth hears that his wife has died and gives a famous speech about the pointlessness of life. A messenger then reports that the forest seems to be moving toward the castle.
The rebel army arrives at Macbeth's castle, throwing down their camouflage branches, and the battle begins.
Macbeth fights fiercely, still believing he cannot be killed. He slays Young Siward, reinforcing his belief that no man can harm him.

Macduff confronts Macbeth. Macbeth boasts that he cannot be killed by any man born of a woman, but Macduff reveals he was born by a cesarean section. Macduff kills Macbeth and brings his head to Malcolm, who is hailed as the new King of Scotland.

A street fight breaks out between the servants of the feuding Capulet and Montague families. The Prince stops it and threatens death to anyone who fights again. Romeo's parents are worried about his sad behavior, and he reveals he is heartbroken over a girl named Rosaline.

Count Paris asks Lord Capulet for permission to marry his daughter Juliet. Capulet says she is too young but invites Paris to a party. Romeo and his friend Benvolio find out about the party and decide to crash it because Rosaline will be there.
Lady Capulet and the Nurse talk to Juliet about the idea of marriage. They tell her that Paris wants to marry her and ask her to consider him at the party tonight.
Romeo, Benvolio, and their witty friend Mercutio approach the Capulet party in disguise. Romeo has a bad feeling about the night, but they go in anyway.

At the party, Romeo sees Juliet and instantly falls in love, completely forgetting Rosaline. Tybalt recognizes Romeo and wants to fight, but Lord Capulet stops him. Romeo and Juliet talk and kiss, only to discover later that they belong to rival families.
After the party, Romeo hides from his friends by jumping a wall into the Capulet orchard, wanting to stay near Juliet.

During the famous balcony scene, Juliet expresses her love for Romeo, unaware he is listening. Romeo reveals himself, and they declare their love for each other, deciding to get married the very next day.

Early the next morning, Romeo visits Friar Laurence and asks him to perform the marriage. The Friar is surprised by how quickly Romeo moved on from Rosaline but agrees, hoping the marriage will end the families' feud.

Mercutio and Benvolio are looking for Romeo. When Romeo arrives, he is in a great mood. Juliet's Nurse comes to find Romeo, and he tells her the plan for Juliet to meet him at Friar Laurence's cell that afternoon to be married.
Juliet waits impatiently for the Nurse to return. When the Nurse finally arrives, she teasingly delays giving Juliet the news, but finally tells her to go to Friar Laurence's cell.
Romeo and Juliet meet at Friar Laurence's cell. The Friar warns them to love moderately, and then leads them away to perform the secret wedding ceremony.

On a hot afternoon, Tybalt confronts Romeo and tries to pick a fight. Romeo, now secretly married to Juliet, refuses to fight his new relative. Angry at Romeo's submission, Mercutio fights Tybalt instead and is killed. In a rage, Romeo kills Tybalt and is banished from Verona by the Prince.

Juliet is excitedly waiting for her wedding night. The Nurse arrives with terrible news, confusing Juliet into initially thinking Romeo is dead. Once she realizes Romeo killed Tybalt and is banished, she is heartbroken but remains loyal to her husband.

Romeo is hiding in Friar Laurence's cell, devastated by his banishment, which he considers worse than death. The Nurse arrives, and the Friar stops Romeo from killing himself, telling him to go to Juliet for the night and then flee to Mantua.
Unaware of Juliet's secret marriage, Lord Capulet decides that marrying Paris will cheer her up after Tybalt's death. He sets the wedding for Thursday.

After spending their only night together, Romeo leaves for Mantua just before dawn. Juliet's parents tell her she must marry Paris. When she refuses, her father furiously threatens to disown her. Even the Nurse advises her to marry Paris, leaving Juliet feeling completely alone.

Juliet goes to Friar Laurence, threatening to kill herself if she has to marry Paris. The Friar gives her a potion that will make her appear dead for 42 hours so she can avoid the wedding and escape with Romeo.
Juliet returns home and pretends to apologize to her father, agreeing to marry Paris. Overjoyed, Lord Capulet moves the wedding up to Wednesday, complicating the Friar's plan.
Alone in her bedroom, Juliet is terrified. She worries the potion might actually be poison or that she will wake up in the family tomb surrounded by dead bodies, but she drinks it anyway for Romeo.
The Capulet household is bustling with activity early the next morning as they prepare for the wedding. Lord Capulet sends the Nurse to wake Juliet.
The Nurse finds Juliet seemingly dead in her bed. Her family and Paris are devastated. Friar Laurence arrives to comfort them and tells them to prepare for a funeral instead of a wedding.
In Mantua, Romeo's servant Balthasar tells him that Juliet is dead. Romeo buys deadly poison from a poor apothecary and immediately sets off for Verona to die beside her.

Friar Laurence learns that his messenger, Friar John, was quarantined due to a plague outbreak and could not deliver the letter explaining the fake death plan to Romeo. Panicking, the Friar rushes to the tomb to get Juliet when she wakes up.

Romeo arrives at the tomb and encounters Paris. They fight, and Romeo kills him. Romeo goes into the tomb, sees Juliet, drinks the poison, and dies. Friar Laurence arrives just as Juliet wakes up. Finding Romeo dead, the Friar flees in terror. Juliet refuses to leave and stabs herself with Romeo's dagger. The Prince and the families arrive, and the tragic deaths finally cause the Montagues and Capulets to end their feud.
